name: Hoces del Alto Ebro and Orbaneja del Castillo Loop
name_original: Valle del Río Ebro y Mirador del Hito — circular desde Santelices
description: This difficult 54.6-mile road cycling route from Santelices offers panoramic views from Mirador del Hito and explores the Ebro River Valley.
region_intro: Road cycling around Merindad De Valdeporres offers diverse terrain across the green landscapes of Burgos. The region features a succession of mountains, rivers, and valleys, providing varied routes for cyclists. Cyclists encounter a mix of mostly paved surfaces, ranging from flat sections to ascents that test endurance. This area is characterized by its natural features, including gorges and the vast karst system of Ojo Guareña. Best road cycling routes around Merindad De Valdeporres The most popular road cycling route is El Escudo and Matanela Passes Loop from Pedrosa, a 52.0 miles (83.7 km) trail that takes 4 hours 33 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gains over mountain passes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Hoces del Alto Ebro and Orbaneja del Castillo Loop, a difficult 54.6 miles (87.9 km) path. This route traverses scenic gorges and offers views of the Ebro river. Local road cyclists also love the Puentedey – Natural Rock Arch of Puentedey loop from Cubillos del Rojo, a 44.6 miles (71.8 km) trail leading through the area around the Natural Rock Arch of Puentedey, often completed in about 3 hours 13 minutes. Road cycling around Merindad De Valdeporres is defined by its varied terrain, including river valleys, mountain passes, and natural rock formations. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging climbs. The routes in Merindad De Valdeporres are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 1,800 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Merindad De Valdeporres's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Merindad De Valdeporres?
A: There are over 70 road cycling routes in Merindad De Valdeporres, offering a diverse range of experiences. These include 7 easy routes, 34 moderate routes, and 37 difficult routes, catering to various fitness levels and preferences.

Q: Are there challenging road bike climbs in the region?
A: Yes, Merindad De Valdeporres features routes with significant elevation gains and challenging climbs. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1058217327/el-escudo-and-matanela-passes-loop-from-pedrosa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Escudo and Matanela Passes Loop from Pedrosa&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 52-mile route that takes on mountain passes, testing endurance with its ascents.

Q: Can I find long-distance road cycling routes in Merindad De Valdeporres?
A: Absolutely. The region offers several long-distance routes for those seeking extended rides. A notable example is the difficult &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31891263" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Covalruyo Viewpoint – Portillo Lunada loop from Soncillo&lt;/a&gt;, which spans over 89 miles (144 km) and includes substantial elevation changes.

Q: What kind of scenic viewpoints can I expect along the routes?
A: The routes in Merindad De Valdeporres often lead to stunning viewpoints, showcasing the region's natural beauty. You can find panoramic views of gorges and river valleys, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6125628" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Ebro Gorge from Alto de los Tornos&lt;/a&gt;, offering memorable vistas during your ride.

Q: Are there any circular road bike routes available?
A: Yes, many routes in Merindad De Valdeporres are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1057563477/hoces-del-alto-ebro-and-orbaneja-del-castillo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hoces del Alto Ebro and Orbaneja del Castillo Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 54.6-mile circular path that traverses scenic gorges and offers views of the Ebro river.

Q: What natural attractions can I explore while road cycling?
A: Merindad De Valdeporres is rich in natural attractions. You can cycle past impressive geological features like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3900715" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Natural Rock Arch of Puentedey&lt;/a&gt;, or explore routes near the vast karst system of Ojo Guareña. The region also features rivers like the Nela and various waterfalls, adding to the picturesque scenery.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by significant landmarks or historical sites?
A: Yes, some routes offer glimpses of historical and cultural landscapes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20161310" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puentedey – Natural Rock Arch of Puentedey loop from Cubillos del Rojo&lt;/a&gt; takes you through the area around the Natural Rock Arch of Puentedey, a notable natural monument. You might also encounter charming villages and old railway lines converted into natural paths.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Merindad De Valdeporres?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from more than 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the region's varied terrain, which includes river valleys, mountain passes, and natural rock formations, providing options for different ability levels and stunning natural beauty.

Q: Are there any routes that feature gorges or canyons?
A: Yes, the region is known for its impressive gorges. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1057563477/hoces-del-alto-ebro-and-orbaneja-del-castillo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hoces del Alto Ebro and Orbaneja del Castillo Loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically traverses scenic gorges. You can also find highlights like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3912343" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Palancas Gorge&lt;/a&gt;, offering dramatic landscapes for your ride.

Q: Are there any routes that include natural rock formations or caves?
A: Merindad De Valdeporres is home to unique geological formations. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40377599" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Sía Pass – Natural Rock Arch of Puentedey loop from Pedrosa&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult route that passes by the Natural Rock Arch of Puentedey. The region also features the vast karst system of Ojo Guareña, with impressive caves like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5679130" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Tirso and San Bernabé Caves&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the general terrain like for road cycling in Merindad De Valdeporres?
A: The terrain for road cycling in Merindad De Valdeporres is diverse, featuring a mix of mostly paved surfaces. You'll encounter everything from flat sections ideal for speed to ascents that test endurance, winding through valleys, quaint villages, and up mountain passes. This variety ensures routes suitable for different cycling preferences.
tips: A medieval town with a beautiful waterfall and an impressive canyon of the Ebro river. Some say that it is the most beautiful town in the province.
A small cave church, directly carved into the sandstone rock. It is very restored and with later additions. Outside the church there are two early medieval tombs carved into the rock.
viewpoint to the entire valley, accessible from the road

The port of Carrales is a mountain pass in the Cantabrian mountain range (Spain), which connects the Burgos municipalities of Alfoz de Bricia, Valle de Valdebezana, and the Cantabrian Valderredible, in the transition zone between the northern sub-plateau and the mountains cantabrian

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this racebike route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as difficult. With an elevation gain of over 1,400 meters and a distance of nearly 88 kilometers, it requires a good to very good level of fitness. Be prepared for sustained ascents, especially when approaching viewpoints like the Mirador del Hito.

faqs.question: Where does the circular route start and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The route is circular, starting and ending in Santelices. While specific parking details for Santelices are not provided, as a village, you can typically find street parking or small public parking areas. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: You can expect diverse terrain suitable for racebikes, primarily on paved surfaces. However, given the nature of the Ebro River Valley and the ascent to a 'Mirador,' there might be varying road conditions. While generally well-maintained, some sections could be rougher, so be prepared for a mix of surfaces.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: Key highlights on this route include the charming village of Orbaneja del Castillo, the historic Rock Church of the Virgin of Carmen, the panoramic Mirador del Hito, and the challenging climb over Puerto de Carrales.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery will I encounter on the ride?
faqs.answer: The route offers varied and picturesque scenery. You'll ride through the Ebro River Valley with views of the winding river, wooded riverbanks, and agricultural lands. The ascent to the Mirador del Hito will reward you with expansive mountain vistas and lush green landscapes, typical of the Cantabrian region.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other cycling or hiking trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route overlaps with several other trails. Notable intersections include sections of the BCB Tres ríos: Por el Valle del Manzanedo, Ebro, Trifón y Nela, Merindades Bike Race 2022, Camino natural de Ebro - Castilla y León, Ruta de los Sentidos, and the Camino Olvidado (Villasante-Bimón).

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to ride this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to ride this route is typically during spring or autumn. These seasons offer milder temperatures, which are ideal for a long and challenging ride with significant elevation gain. Summers can be hot, especially in the Ebro Valley, while winters may bring colder temperatures and potential for adverse weather in higher elevations.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: As a racebike route primarily on public roads and through natural areas, dogs are generally permitted, especially if kept on a leash. However, always be mindful of local regulations, especially when passing through villages or protected areas. Ensure your dog is comfortable with long distances and road cycling environments.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route, especially within the natural park?
faqs.answer: This route passes through the Parque Natural Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón. Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads within Spanish natural parks. However, always respect park regulations, which typically include staying on marked paths, not disturbing wildlife, and proper waste disposal.

faqs.question: Are there places to find refreshments or accommodation near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through villages like Santelices and Orbaneja del Castillo, you should find opportunities for refreshments, such as cafes or small shops. For accommodation, Santelices and other nearby towns offer options ranging from rural guesthouses to small hotels, especially given Santelices' connection to the Camino Olvidado.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this racebike tour?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, you should bring sufficient water and snacks, appropriate cycling gear, a repair kit, and navigation tools. Weather can change, especially in mountainous areas, so pack layers. A helmet is essential, and consider a power bank for your devices.

waypoints.name: Rock Church of the Virgin of Carmen
